definition: to open your eyes and become alert after sleeping; to become aware of a situation or reality
examples: My brother has a hard time waking up in the morning.
She's been asleep for over eleven hours. Should we wake her up?
When did you wake up today?
Wake up! We're going to be late!
I need coffee to wake me up in the morning.
That documentary really woke me up to the dangers of fast food.
etymology_hint: From Old English 'wacan' meaning to wake or arise
notes: Common in daily life. Can mean: (1) to stop sleeping, (2) to become active after laziness, (3) to become aware of something

definition: to leave your bed after sleeping; to rise to a standing position; to ascend
examples: When do you usually get up in the morning?
We got up really late yesterday.
I'm going to try to get up by 7AM.
Mark always gets up early.
Get him up off the floor!
You'd better get up if you don't want to have back problems after sitting for so long.
Get up those stairs right now! It's bath time.
We need to get this refrigerator up to the third floor.
notes: One of the most common phrasal verbs. Main meaning: leave bed in the morning. Also means to stand or ascend.

definition: to open a door, window, or store; to create new opportunities; to make travel or business possible
examples: Could you open up the window? It's a bit warm in here.
I opened the bag up and discovered it was empty.
What time does the store open up?
Open up! It's me!
Learning a second language would open up new possibilities for you.
Putting your business online opens the world up to you.
They've been talking about opening up their travel laws again.
This new foreign policy will open things up for international investors.
Police! Open up!
etymology_hint: Combine 'open' with 'up' for emphasis and different meanings
notes: Can be literal (opening doors) or figurative (creating opportunities). Often used in business and policy contexts.

definition: to sit; to take a seated position; to make someone sit
examples: Thanks for coming. You can sit down wherever you like.
Sit down. You're making me nervous just standing there.
We all sat down at the dinner table.
Do we have enough chairs for everyone to sit down on?
I sat him down because he looked dizzy.
Sit your butt down!
Sit down and relax.
notes: Very common in everyday conversation. Often used as imperative to invite someone to sit.

definition: to stand; to rise from a seated or lying position; to defend yourself verbally; to not meet someone as arranged
examples: He stood up and offered the pregnant woman his seat on the bus.
You should stand up. You've been sitting at your desk for three hours.
Could you help me to stand up? I'm not as young as I used to be.
Ouch! That looked like it hurt. Are you able to stand up?
Stand your brother up. He's being dramatic.
I can't believe he stood you up at the restaurant!
I waited for her for an hour before I realized she had stood me up.
Are you going to let him call you lazy? Stand up for yourself!
I was so proud to see her stand up for herself against her father.
I'm not sure if that fence is strong enough to stand up to strong winds.
Our community needs to stand up to bullying.
etymology_hint: Combine 'stand' with 'up' for different meanings: physical action, defending, or breaking commitment
notes: Multiple meanings: (1) physical - rise to standing, (2) defend yourself verbally (stand up FOR/TO), (3) break a date without telling
